practiceAreas.intro: Whether you're writing your first Will, protecting assets for your children, or administering a loved one's estate, our specialist solicitors will guide you through the options available to you with clarity and compassion.

cards.title: Wills
cards.description: A properly drafted will is the cornerstone of any estate plan. We help you express your wishes with absolute clarity — protecting loved ones, guarding against disputes, and ensuring your intentions stand the test of time.

cards.title: Trusts
cards.description: Trusts offer a flexible vehicle for protecting assets, supporting vulnerable beneficiaries, and managing inheritance tax efficiently. We design and administer trusts tailored to your family's circumstances and objectives.

cards.title: Probate
cards.description: Administering an estate is a profound responsibility, often at a difficult time. Our probate specialists guide executors and families through every stage with patience, precision, and quiet efficiency.

cards.title: Power of Attorney
cards.description: A Lasting Power of Attorney allows your to choose who speaks for you, while you still have the ability to select the person or persons that you trust to carry out your wishes, for your wellbeing and your estate.

whyItMatters.intro: Estate planning is rarely urgent until it is. Each year, thousands of families face avoidable difficulty because of an outdated will, no will at all, or a missing trust arrangement. Here is what proper planning can deliver.

boxes.title: Protect Your Family
boxes.text: Ensure your loved ones, particularly children and dependents, are provided for exactly as you intend, with named guardians and clear bequests.

boxes.title: Reduce Inheritance Tax
boxes.text: Strategic use of trusts, gifts, and reliefs can substantially reduce the IHT burden on your estate — keeping more of what you've built within your family.

boxes.title: Avoid Lengthy Delays
boxes.text: Dying with an ambiguous will can prolong probate by months or years. Clear documentation means a faster, smoother process for those left behind.

boxes.title: Express Personal Wishes
boxes.text: A will is more than a document, it carries your voice. Specific gifts, charitable donations, funeral preferences, all set down precisely to your wishes.

boxes.title: Provide for Blended Families
boxes.text: Second marriages, step-children, and complex family structures require thoughtful provision. Trusts ensure fairness across generations and households.

boxes.title: Lasting Peace of Mind
boxes.text: Above all, knowing your affairs are properly arranged lets you live each day unburdened, and gives your family a final, quiet gift of certainty.

whyUs.headingBefore: Legal services handled with clarity and

columns.title: Fixed fees, no surprises
columns.text: We offer clear fixed-fee options, agreed at the outset. You'll always know what the service costs (with no hidden charges) or percentage-of-service billing.

columns.title: A dedicated, expert legal team
columns.text: Experienced solicitors deal directly with the courts, HMRC, Registry Offices, asset-holders, and you have one named contact throughout.

columns.title: Sensitive & supportive firm
columns.text: We understand whether you're dealing with loss or planning for the future. We move at your pace, explain everything plainly, and treat you and your family with warmth and respect.

items.question: How much does it cost to make a will or trust with Devonshires Claims?
items.answer:
Our fees are fixed and quoted in writing before any work begins. A straightforward single will starts at £xxx plus VAT, and mirror wills for couples from £xxx plus VAT. More complex arrangements — those involving trusts, business assets, or tax planning — are quoted individually after your initial consultation.
We never charge by the hour for standard will drafting, so you always know where you stand.

banner.paragraph: Clear, professional advice from London solicitors who make writing your will straightforward, so your wishes are followed and your family is spared uncertainty.

overview.headingAfter: service covers and how we support you through it.
paragraphs: A will is the single most important document for protecting your family's future. We make sure yours is valid, tax-aware and genuinely reflects your wishes, written in plain English, not legal jargon.

Whether you need a simple will, a pair of mirror wills with your partner, or something more complex involving property, a business or beneficiaries abroad, our solicitors guide you through every decision and handle the drafting end to end.
We also review and update existing wills after life changes such as marriage, divorce, a new child or buying property, and store your signed will securely, free of charge.
checklist: Free secure storage of your signed will

reasons.headingAccent: is the last thing you want
reasons.subtext: Without a valid will, the law decides who inherits, and it rarely matches what you would have wanted.

cards.title: You decide who inherits
cards.text: Choose exactly who receives your home, savings and possessions, rather than the rigid intestacy rules deciding for you.

cards.title: Protect your children
cards.text: Name legal guardians for children under 18 and set out how and when they receive what you leave them.

cards.title: Prevent family disputes
cards.text: A clear, professionally drafted will removes ambiguity, sparing loved ones stress, cost and conflict later.

cards.title: Reduce inheritance tax
cards.text: Use available allowances and reliefs so more of your estate passes to your family, not to HMRC.

whyUs.headingBefore: Wills expertly crafted for you by

columns.title: Fixed, transparent fees
columns.text: You get a clear, fixed price before any work begins. No hourly billing, no surprises, just one agreed fee for your will.

columns.title: Specialist team of solicitors
columns.text: Your will is drafted by a qualified solicitor, many of our team are full members of STEP, the global body for estate-planning practitioners.

columns.title: Sensitive & supportive firm
columns.text: We understand creating a will can be an emotional and personal experience. We move at your pace, explain everything plainly, and treat you with warmth and respect.

journey.subtext: From your first call to the signing of your documents, our process is designed to be measured, transparent, and respectful of the trust you place in us. For example, most clients complete their will in two appointments.
journey.checklistTitle: What to have ready and consider:
checklist: Photo ID and proof of address
A rough list of your assets & liabilities
Any existing will or trust documents

steps.title: Initial Enquiry
steps.text: A friendly, no-obligation telephone or video call. We listen to what matters to you, outline what may be involved, and provide a clear written quote — no jargon, no surprises.

steps.title: Fact-Find Consultation
steps.text: Meeting in person or remotely, your solicitor takes detailed instructions: family circumstances, asset position, wishes for executors and guardians, and any concerns specific to your situation.

steps.title: Draft & Review
steps.text: We prepare a first draft of your will or trust deed and send it for your review. You will have time to read it carefully, share with family if you wish, and raise any questions before we proceed.

steps.title: Signing & Witnessing
steps.text: Once the document is exactly as you want it, you attend our offices to sign in the presence of qualified witnesses. We handle the formalities precisely as required under the Wills Act 1837.

steps.title: Safe Storage & Aftercare
steps.text: We hold your original will in our fireproof strongroom at no charge for life, register it with the National Will Register, and remind you to review it every five years or after major life events.

banner.paragraph: Trusts let you control how, when and to whom your assets pass, shielding what you've built from care fees, divorce, taxation and life's uncertainties. Our specialists make them simple to understand.

overview.headingAfter: does, and how we support you through its creation.
paragraphs: A trust is a legal arrangement that lets one or more trustees hold and manage assets on behalf of the people you want to benefit. It separates legal ownership from benefit, giving you lasting control over money, property and investments — even after you're gone.

We advise on the full range of trusts, recommend the right structure for your goals, and draft the trust deed precisely. Where you'd like ongoing support, our team can also act as professional trustees and handle the administration year to year.
Trusts can be created during your lifetime or written into your will, and are a cornerstone of thoughtful estate and inheritance tax planning.

reasons.subtext: A well-structured trust does something a will alone cannot, it lets you keep guiding your assets for years to come.

cards.title: Control how assets pass
cards.text: Decide exactly when and how beneficiaries receive what you leave — at a certain age, in stages, or at the trustees' discretion.

cards.title: Protect vulnerable people
cards.text: Provide for a child, a disabled relative or someone who can't manage money, without affecting means-tested benefits.

cards.title: Ring-fence your wealth
cards.text: Help shield assets from care-home fees, a beneficiary's divorce, creditors or bankruptcy, keeping them in the family.

cards.title: Plan for tax efficiently
cards.text: Used correctly, trusts can reduce inheritance tax exposure and pass on assets in a more tax-efficient way.

whyUs.headingBefore: Trust expertise you can rely on for the

columns.title: Joined-up estate planning
columns.text: Your trust works alongside your will, powers of attorney and tax position, one coordinated plan, with fixed fees agreed up front.

columns.title: STEP-qualified specialists
columns.text: Trusts are a specialism, not a side-line. Our advisers hold the STEP Diploma and structure trusts day in, day out for UK families.

columns.title: Ongoing trustee support
columns.text: Setting up a trust is just the start. We can act as professional trustees and handle accounts, tax returns and decisions for as long as you need.

journey.checklistTitle: What to have ready and consider:
journey.subtext: From your first conversation to funding your trust, our process is measured, transparent, and respectful of the trust you place in us. Most clients complete their trust in two or three appointments.
checklist: A list of the assets you'd like to protect
Who you want to benefit, and when
Any existing will or trust documents
Details of your wider tax position

steps.title: Initial Enquiry
steps.text: We discuss your assets, your family and what you want a trust to achieve, and explain whether a trust is the right tool, with no obligation.

steps.title: Review your goals & assets
steps.text: A detailed look at your property, investments, beneficiaries and tax position, so our advice is based on the full picture.

steps.title: Structure & draft the deed
steps.text: We set out the most suitable type of trust in plain English, with the pros, costs and tax implications laid out. Your solicitor prepares the trust deed and supporting documents, naming trustees and beneficiaries and defining their powers.

steps.title: Execute & fund the trust
steps.text: We oversee signing and formally transfer the chosen assets into the trust so it's legally effective — including registering it with HMRC.

steps.title: Ongoing administration
steps.text: As professional trustees we can handle accounts, tax returns, distributions and reviews for as long as you need, keeping your trust compliant and effective.

items.question: What exactly is a trust?
items.answer: A trust is a legal arrangement where you (the settlor) transfer assets to trustees, who hold and manage them for the benefit of chosen people (the beneficiaries). It separates who legally owns an asset from who benefits from it, giving you lasting control over how it's used.

banner.paragraph: Losing someone is hard enough without the paperwork. Our probate team handles the legal and financial process with care, so you can take the time you need to grieve without the extra burden.

overview.headingAfter: involves, and how we support you through it.
paragraphs: Probate is the legal process of dealing with the estate of someone who has died by proving the will, settling debts and taxes, and passing what remains to the people entitled to it.

We can take on as much or as little as you'd like and leave the rest to you, while our full estate administration handles everything from valuing assets to making the final distributions, and everything in between.
Throughout, you'll have a single, named contact who keeps you informed, deals with the institutions on your behalf, and treats your family's situation with the sensitivity it deserves.
checklist: Grant of probate & letters of administration

reasons.subtext: Administering an estate carries real legal and financial responsibility. Getting it wrong can be costly, lengthy, and stressful to you and your family.

cards.title: Legal authority to act
cards.text: For most estates you need a grant of probate before banks, pension providers and the Land Registry will release or transfer assets.

cards.title: Protect the executor
cards.text: Executors are personally liable for mistakes. Our guidance shields you from claims, missed creditors and costly errors.

cards.title: Get the tax right, on time
cards.text: Inheritance tax has strict deadlines and penalties. We calculate, report and pay it correctly so nothing is missed.

cards.title: Time, space & less stress
cards.text: We take the admin, phone calls and forms off your hands — giving you room to grieve and support your family.

columns.title: Fixed fees, no surprises
columns.text: We offer clear fixed-fee and grant-only options, agreed at the outset. You'll always know what the service costs (with no hidden charges) or percentage-of-estate billing.

columns.title: A dedicated probate team
columns.text: Experienced probate solicitors deal directly with HMRC, the Probate Registry and asset-holders, and you have one named contact throughout.

columns.title: Sensitive & supportive firm
columns.text: We understand you're dealing with loss. We move at your pace, explain everything plainly, and treat you and your family with warmth and respect.

journey.checklistTitle: What to have ready and consider:
journey.subtext: From your first call to closing the estate, our process is measured, transparent, and respectful of the trust you place in us. Most estates are fully administered within six to twelve months.
checklist: The death certificate and the will (if any)
Details of the deceased's assets & accounts
Names and contact details of beneficiaries
Any lifetime gifts made in the last 7 years

steps.title: Initial Enquiry
steps.text: We talk through what's happened and what's involved, explain your options, and agree a fixed fee — whenever you feel ready to begin.

steps.title: Value the estate
steps.text: We identify and value all assets and debts — property, accounts, investments and possessions — to build a full picture of the estate.

steps.title: Apply for the grant
steps.text: We complete the inheritance tax return, pay any tax due, and apply to the Probate Registry for the grant of probate or letters of administration.

steps.title: Settle debts & taxes
steps.text: Once the grant is issued, we close accounts, settle outstanding debts and bills, and finalise the tax position with HMRC.

steps.title: Distribute assets & close estate
steps.text: We gather in what's owed to the estate and distribute what remains to the beneficiaries, providing clear estate accounts to close everything properly.

items.question: What is probate?
items.answer: Probate is the legal process of administering the estate of someone who has died — proving any will is valid, gathering in their assets, settling debts and taxes, and distributing what's left to the rightful beneficiaries. The official document granting authority to do this is called a "grant of probate".

cards.title: Power of Attorney
cards.text: Secure who speaks on your behalf, when you are unable to do so.

banner.paragraph: A Lasting Power of Attorney lets someone you trust make decisions on your behalf if illness or injury ever means you can't. Our solicitors make putting one in place simple, clear and reassuring.

paragraphs: A Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA) is a legal document that lets you appoint one or more people, your "attorneys", to make decisions for you if you lose the ability to make them yourself. It's one of the most important safeguards you can put in place for later life.

There are two types we advise on. A Property & Financial Affairs LPA covers your money, bank accounts, bills and property. While a Health & Welfare LPA covers your medical care, where you live and day-to-day wellbeing. Most clients choose to make both.
We draft your LPAs precisely, guide you in choosing and appointing the right attorneys, and handle registration with the Office of the Public Guardian from start to finish.
checklist: Registration with the Office of the Public Guardian
Free secure storage of your registered LPAs

reasons.subtext: An LPA can only be made while you have mental capacity. By the time it's needed, it's often too late to create one.

cards.title: Keep decisions in trusted hands
cards.text: You choose exactly who manages your affairs, rather than leaving it to the courts to appoint someone for you.

cards.title: Avoid the Court of Protection
cards.text: Without an LPA, loved ones must apply for deputyship, a process that's slow, costly and overseen by the court.

cards.title: Protect your finances
cards.text: Bills, pensions and property can be managed without disruption if you're ever unable to deal with them yourself.

cards.title: Have your wishes respected
cards.text: Set out your preferences for care and treatment in advance, so the people you trust can honour what matters to you.

columns.title: Fixed, transparent fees
columns.text: One clear price agreed before we start, covering drafting and our work on registration. The OPG's registration fee is the only separate cost.

columns.title: Drafted to avoid rejection
columns.text: A surprising number of DIY LPAs are rejected over small errors. We draft and check every detail so yours is accepted by the OPG without delay.

columns.title: Advice on choosing attorneys
columns.text: Picking the right attorneys, and replacements, is the most important decision. We help you weigh it up and tailor instructions to your wishes.

journey.checklistTitle: What to have ready and consider:
journey.subtext: From your first conversation to registration, our process is measured, transparent, and reassuring. Most clients complete their LPAs in two straightforward appointments.
checklist: Your ID and proof of address
Who you'd like to appoint as attorneys
Someone to act as your certificate provider
Details of the people to be notified (if any)

steps.title: Free case assessment
steps.text: A relaxed conversation about your circumstances, which type of LPA you need and who you might appoint. We confirm a fixed fee before you decide.

steps.title: Choose your attorneys
steps.text: We help you decide who should act, whether jointly or separately, and whether to name replacements, then capture any instructions and preferences.

steps.title: We draft your LPAs
steps.text: Your solicitor prepares the documents accurately, acts as your certificate provider where appropriate, and sends everything to you to review.

steps.title: Sign & witness
steps.text: We oversee correct signing by you, your attorneys and witnesses in the right order, so the LPAs are valid and ready to register.

steps.title: Register with the OPG
steps.text: We submit your LPAs to the Office of the Public Guardian, track them through the process, and store your registered originals safely.

items.question: What is a Lasting Power of Attorney?
items.answer: An LPA is a legal document that lets you (the "donor") appoint one or more trusted people (your "attorneys") to make decisions on your behalf if you're no longer able to make them yourself, for example through illness, injury or loss of mental capacity.
